one_paragraph_verdict: This paper is a direct empirical and theoretical counter-argument to the techno-feudalism thesis, specifically using Amazon as a primary case study. It directly engages the core distinctions of the theory map—specifically the rent-vs-profit distinction and the producer-vs-rentier characterization—to argue that Amazon's dominance is a result of intensified capitalist competition and massive investment in the forces of production (logistics and cloud infrastructure) rather than the extraction of feudal rents. By providing firm-level financial metrics (ROIC, operating margins) and analyzing R&D expenditure, the paper provides the exact type of 'real evidence' required to support the claim that tech giants remain capitalist producers.
contextual_relevance: Critique of Lina Khan's antitrust framework, which provides the regulatory context for the broader debate on platform power.
Discussion of Amazon's role as a gateway for Chinese manufacturers, touching on the geopolitical dimensions of global supply chains.

arguments_for_theory.argument: Amazon's high R&D spending and massive capital investment in logistics and AWS are incompatible with the 'lazy rentier' model of feudalism; these are markers of intense capitalist competition to reduce circulation time.

The distinction between 'commercial profit' (return for circulation services) and 'monopoly rent' is critical; Amazon's fees from 3P sellers reflect the productive value of its logistics infrastructure (FBA) rather than the extraction of rent.

Amazon's profitability (ROIC and operating margins) converges with the average of leading US firms, empirically challenging the claim that platforms generate 'super-profits' or rents that signal a systemic transition to feudalism.

Amazon's dominance is driven by the compression of 'time-space' (reducing circulation costs) through physical infrastructure, rather than the 'user-ism' focus on data extraction as the primary locus of value.

what_article_cannot_adjudicate: The specific intellectual genealogy of the techno-feudalism thesis rooted in the Brenner-Wallerstein debate (C2).
The theoretical integration of expropriation and exploitation into an 'expanded conception of capitalism' (C5).
The constitutive role of the US national security state in the development of Silicon Valley (C4).
state_capital_nexus_relevance: Marginal; the paper focuses on the internal logic of capitalist competition and circulation rather than the state's constitutive role in building tech hegemony.
